title: Re: Quick Q about GitHub connector / can we do a short demo?
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam@openhooks.dev>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Interest in GitHub connector / quick integration questions

Hey Ava,

Signed up for Redwood this morning (loving the quick onboarding — neat product!). I work on the integrations team at OpenHooks and we're evaluating a few inference providers to power a code-intent assistant we want to ship into the developer experience on GH. I had a couple quick questions before we book a demo: 

- Can the GitHub connector be scoped to specific orgs/repos and only enabled for certain teams?
- How does repo access work for private repos (PAT vs app installation)?
- Any constraints with Actions workflows (we want inference calls triggered by PR comments and workflow runs)?

Would appreciate a short 20–30m demo so we can walk through the integration pattern and security model. I'm on Pacific time and flexible most afternoons this week.

Quick bits about us: OpenHooks (YC '24) — we run a hosted service with some enterprise customers on GH Enterprise Server.

Thanks,
Liam
Maintainer, OpenHooks
liam@openhooks.dev
https://openhooks.dev


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam@openhooks.dev>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>, Diego Fernandez <diego_fernandez@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 May 2027 09:38:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Interest in GitHub connector / quick integration questions

Hi Liam — great to meet you and welcome to Redwood!

Thanks for the questions — we can absolutely scope connectors to orgs/repos and limit access by GitHub teams. For private repos we support both App installations (recommended for org-wide installs) and PAT-based approaches for one-off repos; on complex enterprise installs we usually recommend App installation + an allowlist. Actions-triggered calls are supported — we expose a tiny runtime shim you can drop into workflows so you don't ship tokens into CI.

Would love to do a 25–30 minute call to walk through the pattern and the security model. I'm available:
- Wed 19 May 10:30–11:30 PT
- Wed 19 May 15:00–16:00 PT
- Thu 20 May 13:00–15:00 PT

If none of those work, feel free to pick a time on my calendar: https://cal.redwood.ai/ava/30min

I've attached a short prep checklist that covers the pieces we'll demo (scoping, auth options, sample Actions snippet). I'll loop in Jonas (solutions) and Diego (product engineer) so we can get into specifics if you want.

Best,
Ava Chen
SDR, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: integration-prep-checklist.pdf (6KB)]


From: Liam O'Connor <liam@openhooks.dev>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 19 May 2027 08:01:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Interest in GitHub connector / quick integration questions

Hey Ava — thanks for the fast reply and helpful notes. That schedule looks good but I have a conflict at 10:30 PT. Could we do Wed 19 May at 15:00 PT (that works for our London folks too)?

Couple of specifics that would be great to cover on the call (so your engineer can prep):
- Example of GitHub App installation flow and the minimum permissions required
- How to route inference requests so we can distinguish calls coming from Actions vs webhooks
- Token rotation / audit logs for customer installs

Also dropping a repo link you can look at to see our pattern: https://github.com/openhooks/intent-prototypes (public)

Liam


From: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam@openhooks.dev>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>, Diego Fernandez <diego_fernandez@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 19 May 2027 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Interest in GitHub connector / quick integration questions

Hi Liam — Jonas here (Solutions). Thanks for the repo link — useful context.

Confirmed: we booked Wed 19 May 15:00 PT. Details/quick agenda: 
- 15:00–15:05 PT: Intros and goals (Ava + Liam)
- 15:05–15:20 PT: Diego walks through GitHub App vs PAT pattern and shows sample Actions snippet
- 15:20–15:30 PT: Q&A on logs/audit, token rotation, and rollout plan

Zoom: https://zoom.us/j/9876543210?pwd=example
Calendar invite sent (please accept) — I included Diego for the deep technical Qs. If you'd prefer a different time, let us know and we can reschedule.

> Liam

title: Privacy roadmap: DPA intake, SCC module check, controller/processor clarification
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marta Kovac <marta.kovac@fidelityhealth.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 14 May 2027 14:10:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo recap + next steps on DPA/SCC questions

Hi Marta — great to meet you and Igor on the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we can keep momentum for the procurement/legal review at FidelityHealth.

Summary (short):
- Redwood is happy to support FidelityHealth as a data controller for customer-provided data. We recognize the importance of clarifying roles before formalizing a DPA.
- You asked about SCC module preference and whether the standard EU SCCs or the new modular approach is acceptable for your legal team.
- SOC 2 status: we shared that Redwood has a rolling Type II audit in progress; I’ve called out timing below.

Questions/action items we need from you:
1) Confirm whether FidelityHealth will be signing as Controller for the product (it sounded like yes on today’s call).
2) Please confirm whether your legal team has a strong preference for the SCC module flavor (we can provide both Module 2/Module 3 mapping guidance).
3) Security contact for the vendor security questionnaire (DSQ) so we can kick off scoping and deliver the initial answers in 3 business days.

What I’m attaching here: Redwood DPA template (editable), a short SCC variant matrix to show typical mapping, and a scope worksheet for the DSQ. If your legal prefers to mark up our DPA, we can review and follow with a DocuSign-ready agreement.

Proposed next steps:
- You (or legal) confirm controller/processor roles and SCC module preference (reply to this thread).
- We will populate the DSQ and send a first-pass response + evidence links within 72 hours.
- Redwood legal to prepare DocuSign link for the DPA once your counsel has marked up or accepted the template.

Thanks again — happy to jump on a short follow-up call with your legal or security teams if helpful.

Best,
Ava Chen
Enterprise AE | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0142

Attachments:
- redwood_dpa_template.pdf (PDF, DPA template)
- redwood_scc_matrix.csv (CSV, SCC module mapping)
- vendor_security_q_scope.xlsx (XLSX, DSQ scope worksheet)

From: Marta Kovac <marta.kovac@fidelityhealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Igor Petrov <igor.petrov@fidelityhealth.com>, FidelityHealth Legal <legal@fidelityhealth.com>
Date: Fri, 14 May 2027 17:03:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on DPA/SCC questions

Hi Ava — thanks for the clear recap. A few clarifications from our side after a quick internal sync:

1) Controller/Processor: FidelityHealth will be signing as Controller for customer data we upload; Redwood will be Processor for those flows. Please include that as the starting assumption in the DPA.

2) SCC module: our legal team prefers the modular SCC approach (Module 2 where Redwood processes on our behalf), but they will want to see the mapping of subprocessors and the cross-border transfer mechanics.

3) SOC 2: we rely heavily on SOC 2 Type II reports. Can you confirm your current state and anticipated completion date for a Type II report? We need an ETA and whether any interim evidence is available now.

4) DSQ: Please start the vendor security questionnaire — our security lead Igor will own the technical review. His contact is igor.petrov@fidelityhealth.com. Send initial answers and mark any gaps.

Small ask on timing: can you get the initial DSQ answers + the DPA (marked with controller/processor roles) to us by EOW (Monday)? Our procurement kickoff is scheduled for Tuesday and having those docs will help.

Thanks — Marta
Marta Kovac
Sr. Procurement Manager | FidelityHealth
+marta.kovac@fidelityhealth.com

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marta Kovac <marta.kovac@fidelityhealth.com>
Cc: Igor Petrov <igor.petrov@fidelityhealth.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, FidelityHealth Legal <legal@fidelityhealth.com>
Date: Sat, 15 May 2027 09:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on DPA/SCC questions

Thanks Marta — happy to confirm next steps and timing. Per internal alignment:

- Controller/Processor: we'll mark Redwood as Processor and FidelityHealth as Controller in the DPA template we attached.
- SCC module: attached CSV maps our standard Module 2 mapping to common contract clauses and subprocessors. If legal prefers Module 3 variants we can adapt quickly.
- SOC 2: Redwood has completed SOC 2 Type I (2025) and is currently in a rolling Type II audit cycle — expected issuance: Aug 2027. In the meantime we can provide interim evidence: our control matrix, penetration test summary (redacted), and the SOC2 audit kickoff letter. Links/attachments below.

Files we are sharing on this thread (initial package):
- initial_dsq_answers.docx (first-pass DSQ answers, marked TODOs)
- redwood_dpa_template.pdf (DPA with roles pre-marked)
- redwood_scc_matrix.csv (SCC module mapping)
- vendor_security_q_scope.xlsx (scope worksheet we used on the call)

Logistics/links:
- HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/1234567/DEAL-9876 (we will update stage to "Legal Review")
- DocuSign DPA (draft): https://app.docusign.com/documents/0A1B2C3D (will update after your counsel reviews)
- Drive folder w/ artifacts: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3cRedwood-FH-Privacy

Next steps (I will own coordination):
1) Neha will complete the DSQ gaps and add evidence links by EOD Monday.
2) Lauren will coordinate with my team and Redwood Legal to accept or track counsels’ marks in the DocuSign draft.
3) If Igor or your security team wants a short technical follow-up we can do a 30-minute call early next week — suggest Tue morning (PDT)/Tue afternoon (ET).

Let me know if that timeline works.

Best,
Ava

Attachments referenced: initial_dsq_answers.docx; redwood_dpa_template.pdf; redwood_scc_matrix.csv; vendor_security_q_scope.xlsx

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marta Kovac <marta.kovac@fidelityhealth.com>, Igor Petrov <igor.petrov@fidelityhealth.com>
Date: Sat, 15 May 2027 10:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on DPA/SCC questions

Quick internal note — I’ll own the DSQ follow-up and will:
- Fill in the remaining DSQ items marked TODO and attach the redacted pen test summary to the drive folder.
- Add a short table of subprocessors and data flows to the SCC matrix so their legal team can see where cross-border transfers occur.
- Target: EOD Monday (17 May) for the complete packet to send to Marta/Igor.

I also propose we prepare a 20-min tech walk for Igor (scope: data flows, retention, encryption at rest/in transit, access controls). Ava — can you schedule Tue 10:00 PDT if that works for their team?

-Neha
Security & Trust | Redwood Inference

title: Pilot DPA: scoped no-improve carveout + prompt/response ownership confirmation
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@cerebellumhealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@cerebellumhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 10 May 2027 09:12:00 -0400
Subject: Pilot DPA draft — can we get a scoped 'no-improve' plus prompt ownership confirmed?

Hi Ava / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week — team was impressed. Procurement asked me to send our standard DPA redline for a two-month pilot. Two items we need to confirm before we circulate to our legal:

1) No-model-improvement / no-training commitment for pilot data (we'd like a firm NO-IMPROVE clause and an ops proof path).
2) Explicit ownership of prompts/responses: Cerebellum needs a clear statement that generated outputs and submitted prompts remain our IP and will not be used to improve models.

I attached a redline (Cerebellum_DPA_draft_v3.pdf). Can you confirm operationally: onshore routing, KMS residency, and whether Redwood can provide an evidence packet (logs + config) at pilot end?

If easier, we can sign a short addendum — happy to fast-track.

Best,
Liam
Director of AI Platform
Cerebellum Health

Attachments: Cerebellum_DPA_draft_v3.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@cerebellumhealth.com>
Cc: David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 10 May 2027 12:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Pilot DPA draft — can we get a scoped 'no-improve' plus prompt ownership confirmed?

Hi Liam — thanks for sending this over and for the clear asks. Looping in David (privacy/ops) and Marissa (account lead) so we can respond to both legal and procurement items. Quick summary up front: we can support a scoped no-improve commitment for the pilot with operational evidence; there are a few implementation details to confirm.

Requested next steps from our side:
- David: confirm the evidence artifacts we can provide and timeline.
- Marissa: align on contract path (addendum vs redline) and procurement contact.

A few clarifying questions for Cerebellum to speed review:
- Will the pilot data include PHI or regulated identifiers? (affects onshore requirement)
- Expected volume/peak qps and retention window for logs (we can adjust sampling if needed).

We reviewed the attached PDF briefly; I added inline comments and a short redline (redlines-ava_comments.docx). We aim to return a proposed addendum (1 page) that captures: NO-IMPROVE (pilot only), prompt/response ownership statement, and a defined ops-evidence deliverable at pilot completion.

I can jump on a 30-min call tomorrow if that helps.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

Attachments: redlines-ava_comments.docx


From: David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@cerebellumhealth.com>
Date: Tue, 11 May 2027 08:37:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Pilot DPA draft — operational feasibility and evidence artifacts

On Mon, May 10, 2027 at 12:03 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Hi Liam — thanks for sending this over and for the clear asks. Looping in David (privacy/ops) and Marissa (account lead) so we can respond to both legal and procurement items.

Thanks Ava — quick operational answers below. Short version: we can meet the pilot asks with these constraints and artifacts.

Operational constraints / commitments:
- No-model-improvement (pilot only): We will disable downstream model tuning/adaptive learning for the tenant and enforce it via serving config flag (tenant.improve=false).
- Data residency: For US-only onshore routing, we will pin the tenant to our us-west region pool; data and logs remain in-region.
- KMS: We can integrate with customer-managed keys in AWS KMS (bring-your-own-key) for request-level encryption; proof of key ownership will be a policy audit entry.

Evidence artifacts we can provide at pilot end (deliverables):
1) Configuration snapshot (tenant settings file) — confirms improve=false and region assignment.
2) Minimal audit log extract (redacted) showing no training calls and routing info. Note: full raw logs are subject to retention policy; we'll provide sampled exports aligned with your retention ask.
3) KMS policy snapshot + key ID (ops-proof-kms-diagram.png attached) showing onshore key binding.

Notes / caveats:
- We cannot provide model internals or training datasets (we're disabling training hooks; there will be no derivative model produced but we do not produce training dataset artifacts).
- If the pilot includes any PHI, we need a signed DPA amendment referencing required safeguards; PHI changes sampling/retention defaults.

I'll prepare the evidence pack template — ETA Wed EOD. If Cerebellum prefers DocuSign addendum, indicate a signer and we'll route.

-David
David Kim
Head of Privacy & Compliance | Redwood Inference

Subject: Marketplace pilot — short, urgent ask on logs/backups

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo last week — BrightMarket is ready to run the two-week marketplace integration pilot starting next Monday (5/17). Before we flip the switch, our security team asked for two narrow exceptions to the standard retention schedule so we can debug historic transaction reconciliation and run fraud analysis during the pilot window.

Requests (timeboxed to the pilot):
- Keep request/response logs associated with marketplace ordering flows for 90 days (instead of Redwood's default 30).
- Delay purging of snapshot backups for pilot-related resources to 120 days before deletion (instead of the usual 30/45 cadence).

We realize this is an exception — we've attached a small CSV mapping of the pilot datasets so you can scope it (pilot-data-map.csv).

Couple of notes:
- We must ensure this is timeboxed and limited to the pilot-specific dataset IDs listed in the CSV.
- Data will include EU resident records; our legal team will need a short DPA amendment and subprocessors list that covers any cross-border processing.

Can your team confirm feasibility and what minimal evidence you need (SOC2 summary, signed amendment, PO)? We're hoping to have confirmation by EOD Friday (5/14).

Thanks,
Elena

Head of Marketplace Integrations
BrightMarket

Elena — thanks for the clear scope and the CSV; appreciate the short turnaround.

Quick response: we can likely support a pilot-scoped exception but need Compliance and Legal to sign off on: (1) the exact dataset IDs, (2) retention window/timebox, and (3) the DPA amendment language for cross-border handling.

title: Tail latency budgeting & GPU mapping — RFI response
preview:
From: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: infra-ops@acmehealth.com
Date: 2027-05-01T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the quick intake call this morning. Attached is a short extract from our RFP that focuses on availability and tail-latency commitments. Our infra team is especially concerned about P95 vs P99 tail behavior for short prompt workloads (<=32 tokens output) and large context chat sessions (up to 8k tokens).

Specific asks:
- For reserved capacity, can you indicate per-GPU tail latency distributions (P50/P95/P99) for both A100-80GB and H100-80GB under our workload mix (see attachment)?
- How do you map failover to lower-cost GPUs (e.g., L4 / T4 class) while protecting tail latency and correctness?
- Do you have recommended reservation blocks for mixed workloads (bursty short prompts + sustained long-context chat) that minimize wasted reserved hours?

We'd appreciate a short written reply and a workbook we can use to model our reservations.

Best,
Sophie
Sophie Miller
Head of Infra Ops — Acme Health

Attachments: acme_tail_latency_requirements.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-01T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Sophie —

Thanks for sending this over. I'm looping in Marcus (runtime perf) and Priyom (capacity planning) to provide the numbers and a workbook you can drop your mixes into. Expect the initial write-up by EOD Monday. A few clarifying notes:

- When you say "protecting tail latency and correctness" for fallback GPUs, do you mean strict P99 guarantees or an SLO that allows graceful degradation with a capped number of degraded requests that return a compatible model variant?
- For your short-prompt mix, can you confirm the average batch size and concurrency target (we can model 1:1 concurrency and optimistic batching separately)?

I'll send a meeting invite if that would be helpful, but we'll aim to reply in the thread first.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T14:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Hi Sophie —

We ran quick microbenchmarks and modeled two representative profiles from your attachment:

Profile A: short prompts (<=32 output tokens), median input length ~30 tokens, concurrency target 200 qps
Profile B: long-context chat (8k context, 256 token outputs), concurrency target 30 qps

High-level results (single-request, cold KV cache):
- A100-80GB (fp16 / quant-aware kernels): P50 18ms, P95 42ms, P99 95ms for Profile A at 200 qps with continuous batching tuned to 80ms latency budget.
- H100-80GB (bf16): P50 13ms, P95 30ms, P99 68ms for Profile A — H100 shows consistently lower tail due to faster attention kernels and larger L2.
- For Profile B (long-context), both GPUs see higher medians: A100 P50 120ms / P95 260ms / P99 420ms; H100 P50 95ms / P95 210ms / P99 360ms.

Fallbacks and mapping guidance:
- We do not recommend falling back to L4/T4 for latency-sensitive P99 targets on short prompts unless you accept a higher P99 or perform model quantization + distilled variants. L-class GPUs are cost-effective for background batch/embedding workloads but have higher variance for low-latency web requests.
- Preferred strategy: reserve a primary pool on H100 for latency SLOs and maintain a small warm A100 pool as an intermediate tier. Use policy-based routing: route high-priority traffic to H100; non-critical low-cost requests to L4.
- Graceful degradation: automatic model-variant fallback (same-architecture distilled model) plus optional chunked response streaming to hide latency spikes.

Reservation heuristics we recommend (starter plan for your mixed workload):
- H100 pool: size to reach sustained 30 qps for long-context + headroom for 20% burst (estimate 8 H100 nodes).
- A100 pool: cover remaining short-prompt capacity for steady-state 180 qps (estimate 6 A100 nodes), configured with aggressive batching window and prefix caching.
- On-call burst policy: enable 12-hour burst capacity using shared GPU burst credits to handle unexpected spikes without overcommitting reserved hours.

I've attached a blank capacity workbook you can use to plug in your exact qps, concurrency, and percent-burst assumptions. The workbook includes sheets for kernel mix, per-model token cost, and tail-latency budget allocation.

Happy to walk through numbers live.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Senior Runtime Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx


From: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: infra-ops@acmehealth.com
Date: 2027-05-06T08:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Thanks Marcus — this is very helpful. A couple follow-ups after reviewing the workbook: 

1) For the H100 pool you recommend "8 nodes" for long-context. Can you surface the utilization threshold you used to size that? Is that 70% GPU util or 50%? We need to map to our SLA burn-rate for reserved hours.
2) If we enable auto-burst credits for 12 hours, how do you meter and bill that? Is burst billed at reserved rate or on-demand?
3) We're curious about tail-latency isolation: if one tenant in our multi-tenant namespace floods the prefix cache and causes KV miss storms, how does Redwood surface/limit that to meet P99 for others?

Would appreciate short answers inline and we can reconvene if needed.

Best,
Sophie


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-09T16:38:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Sophie — quick inline replies and a couple of examples from the workbook.

1) Sizing utilization assumption: we sized H100 nodes to an average target of ~60% sustained GPU util for long-context sessions. That gives ~25% headroom for KV-cache churn and unexpected attention kernel variance while keeping reserved-hour efficiency reasonable. You can change the sheet to 50%/70% to see cost impact; the workbook has those toggles.

2) Billing for burst credits: burst is billed at the committed reserved rate up to your committed capacity; additional burst beyond your committed burst allowance is billed at on-demand pricing. The workbook's "Billing" tab models 12-hour daily burst allowance vs ad-hoc burst, and outputs monthly cost estimates. We also include an example contract clause for enterprise agreements to cap burst overage.

3) Tail-latency isolation & KV storms: engineering controls we deploy:
   - Per-tenant prefix cache quotas and eviction priorities; if a tenant triggers a high miss rate we'll throttle that tenant's new prefix caching for a short recovery window.
   - Request-level QoS: mark SLO-critical requests and route them to isolated worker pools with reserved KV cache regions.
   - Real-time alerts: when miss rate for a tenant exceeds threshold, we emit an auto page to infra-ops and move the tenant to a protected pool until manual review.

Attached is a filled example scenario using your sample traffic numbers. If you want, we can run your real traffic trace in our sandbox to produce a drop-in sizing recommendation.

Next steps:
- I'll share the completed workbook with sample toggles and a short 30-minute walkthrough (optional).
- Ava, can you coordinate contracting touchpoints if Acme wants to pursue a Dedicated commitment?

Thanks,
Priyom Das
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx (scenario example)

   - Real-time alerts: when miss rate for a tenant exceeds threshold, we emit an auto page to infra-ops and move the tenant to a protected pool until manual review.

Attached is a filled example scenario using your sample traffic numbers. If you want, we can run your real traffic trace in our sandbox to produce a drop-in sizing recommendation.

Next steps:
- I'll share the completed workbook with sample toggles and a short 30-minute walkthrough (optional).
- Ava, can you coordinate contracting touchpoints if Acme wants to pursue a Dedicated commitment?

Thanks,
Priyom Das
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx (scenario example)

title: Temporary SLO accommodation request — dedicated pool migration (Solaris Energy)
preview:
From: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Meera Patel <meera.patel@solarisen.com>
Date: Mon, 03 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window

Hi Ava,

We’re kicking off a planned model reindex and dataset backfill next month that will shift a large portion of inference traffic into a short window (estimated peak ~8x our baseline for ~36 hours). The migration is customer-driven (internal analytics backfill + embeddings regen) and can’t be fully throttled without impacting regulatory reporting.

We want to avoid triggering Dedicated pool SLO breaches during the window. Can Redwood consider a temporary SLO accommodation or explicit carveout for the migration window (we’re targeting May 20–22 UTC)? If an accommodation isn’t possible, please outline the mitigations you can enact (pre-warm, burst capacity, prioritized queues) and what commercial remediation would look like if we get a breach.

Attached is a high-level migration timeline (preview) — I can send a full runbook once we lock dates.

Thanks,
Derek Shaw
Director of Platform Engineering
Solaris Energy

Attachment: Solaris_migration_preview.pdf

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 03 May 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window

Hi Derek — thanks for the heads-up and the preview. We can support Solaris during a short, planned migration, but we need a clear migration window, runbook, and an agreed mitigation + commercial plan. Quick notes: 

- Technical mitigations we can apply: pre-warm reserved nodes, enable temporary burst slices on your Dedicated pool, and create a migration-priority queue with explicit concurrency caps to limit cross-tenant impact.
- Operational constraints: we cannot compromise baseline throughput for unrelated customer traffic; any temporary carveout will be time-limited and instrumented.
- Commercial: typical approach is an SLA addendum + one-time invoice credit tied to the migration window.

I’ve looped in Markus and Karthik to capture technical and commercial constraints. I’m attaching our SLO_Waiver_Template_v1.pdf so we can iterate quickly — please send the detailed runbook and confirm the exact UTC window you’d like us to cover. Proposed next step: you send the runbook by EOD May 7, we’ll return a config + credit proposal by May 10.

Best,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Accounts — Dedicated Capacity
Redwood Inference

Attachment: SLO_Waiver_Template_v1.pdf

From: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 08:32:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window — technical proposal

Ava / team — wrote up a concise technical mitigation we can propose to Solaris. Key points: 

1) Dedicated pool behaviour: we use per-customer request queues with token-bucket shaping. By default we reserve a baseline capacity of GPUs and throughput that maps to the committed concurrency.

2) Migration carveout approach:
   - Reserve baseline capacity = 60% of current committed throughput for production-critical traffic.
   - Allow controlled burst window: configure a temporary \"migration slice\" that permits up to 3x nominal tokens/sec for 48 hours, split into rolling 6-hour ramp windows to avoid immediate tail pressure.
   - Enforce per-request priority: migration jobs assigned lower priority but with guaranteed minimal concurrency so they don’t cause head-of-line blocking.

3) Queueing/behavioral safeguards:
   - Tail-drop protection on best-effort queue to prevent eviction of high-priority inference traffic.
   - KV-cache pre-warm on a subset of nodes to reduce token compute overhead during peak.
   - Real-time metrics stream + alerting (latency p50/p95/p99, token/sec, queue depth) — we’ll create a shared Grafana board and Slack channel.

4) Risk and rollback: if we see cross-tenant latency regressions >15% sustained for 5 minutes, the migration slice is automatically scaled down and we fall back to baseline routing.

I’ve attached a minimal patch for queueing config to apply (queueing_config_patch.diff). If Solaris agrees to the ramp schedule we can pre-stage the config on May 18 and flip it on the morning of May 20 UTC.

— Markus
Senior Runtime Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: queueing_config_patch.diff

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Derek Shaw <derek.shaw@solarisen.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 06 May 2027 16:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO accommodation for migration window — commercial terms

Derek — following Ava/Markus’ notes, here’s a commercial proposal for the migration addendum: 

- Duration: 48 hours (May 20 00:00 UTC — May 22 00:00 UTC) with optional 12-hour rollback buffer.
- Technical guardrails: as Markus outlined (60% baseline reservation + 3x controlled burst with ramping).
- Compensation: one-time credit of 12% of the monthly Dedicated invoice proportional to the migration window (applied to the next billing cycle).
- SLA addendum & signature: we’ll send an SLA addendum that captures the temporary carveout, rollback triggers, monitoring obligations, and the credit calculation.

I’m including a DocuSign link for the addendum — if you agree, sign and we’ll schedule the staging run on May 18. If Solaris needs a different credit level we can discuss with Finance, but this is our standard mitigated-waiver offer for large but short migrations.

DocuSign: https://docusign.net/Signing/Start/ABCDEF123456

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@clearpointbank.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@clearpointbank.com>, Eli Turner <eli.turner@auditpartner.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: ClearPoint evaluation — security gates and timeline

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Our security team wants to formalize a short MAP so we can evaluate Redwood for a production pilot. High-level asks from our side: SOC 2 attestation (or timeline to ship evidence), SIG/CAIQ responses, an initial DPA we can redline, and confirmation of data retention/deletion SLAs for caching and logs.

Proposed cadence: kickoff the week of May 9, SIG bulk review the following week, DPA redline by end of week 3, SOC2 evidence review week 4. Can you share a short runbook/MAP with owners on your side and any standard documents you will attach?

Attaching our internal primer to give context.

Thanks,
Anna
-----
ClearPoint Security Primer (attachment): ClearPoint_Security_Primer.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@clearpointbank.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-02T11:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: ClearPoint evaluation — security gates and timeline

Anna — thanks for the clear ask and the primer. Attached is a concise MAP + runbook and our baseline artifacts to accelerate your review. High level plan below (happy to iterate):

- Week 0 (May 9): Kickoff call — agenda: scope, sequence lengths, data residency, and acceptance criteria. Owner: Ava (AM).
- Week 1 (May 10–14): SIG/CAIQ Q&A bulk delivery — Owner: Marcus (runtime/security tech lead). We'll provide an initial completed CAIQ spreadsheet and highlighted items for you to focus on.
- Week 2 (May 17–21): DPA draft + procurement coordination — Owner: Naomi (legal ops). We will deliver DPA_draft_v1.docx for redline and propose standard indemnities/retention clauses.
- Week 3 (May 24–28): SOC 2 evidence & operational questions — Owner: Ben (compliance). We will surface a redacted SOC 2 summary and evidence index.
- Week 4 (May 31–Jun 4): Acceptance review and go/no-go decision.

Key security touchpoints to confirm now:
- Data retention for KV cache and ephemeral runtime state (we propose TTL=30 days by default, configurable per-customer).
- Log retention: we retain operational logs for 12 months; customer-managed log exports available on request.
- DPA: we support deletion requests and can add explicit SLA language for deletion and attestations.

Attachments: Redwood_SOC2_summary.pdf, DPA_draft_v1.docx, SIG-CAIQ-responses.xlsx

Links: https://docs.redwood.ai/shared/MAP-ClearPoint-2027 (MAP draft)

Next steps: confirm kickoff slot (I suggested Tue May 10 at 10am PT in HubSpot) and let us know if you need a private deployment discussion up front.

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | (415) 555-0182


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@clearpointbank.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T08:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: ClearPoint evaluation — security gates and timeline

Adding a few tech notes for the SIG call and MAP so we don't get downstream surprises:

- KV cache/data retention: our default TTL is 30 days but we can offer 0-day (no cache) or shorter windows for sensitive workloads; explain tradeoffs to the ClearPoint security team on latency/cost.
- Audit logs: we capture request/response hashes and runtime metadata; payloads are not stored unless explicitly enabled via integrations. Retention is configurable up to 24 months for enterprise.
- Private deployment option: if they need additional network isolation, our Private VPC option supports HSM/KMS integration and can be included in the MAP as an optional milestone.

I'll prepare the CAIQ notes and highlight any control gaps and compensating controls for the SIG review.

Marcus
Principal Engineer, Serving Runtime

title: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>
To: Legal <legal@edgehealth.com>, security@edgehealth.com
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions

Hi team — thanks again for taking the time on Friday. Per our call, I wanted to start a quick thread to capture two narrow items EdgeHealth asked about so we can get answers back to legal/security.

1) KMS/export-controls: Edge asked whether customer-managed keys can be used for PII and whether any subprocessors will ever have access to plaintext during model serving or logs.
2) Subprocessor reachability & dataflow: they’d like a concise inventory and a diagram that shows which subprocessors touch identifiable data, where it is encrypted, and whether keys or tokenized blobs cross third-party boundaries.

I’ve attached our current subprocessor inventory and a draft dataflow PDF (v2) that I pulled together after the demo. Neha — could you please confirm the following by EOD Wednesday?
- Does Redwood ever persist unencrypted input or output to disk outside customer-controlled KMS boundaries? (short answer)
- Which subprocessors, if any, would see decrypted payloads for troubleshooting or pipeline jobs?
- If OrbitCloud (our optional hosting partner in some regions) is in the serving path, do they ever have access to keys or plaintext?

Marissa — can you include a quick paragraph on our standard DPA wording for KMS + key custody (a one-paragraph summary for the legal team to consume)?

I’ll loop OrbitCloud if we need a confirmation from their side.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: redwood_subprocessor_inventory_2027-05.csv (csv, 32KB); edgehealth_dataflow_v2.pdf (pdf, 420KB)

From: Legal <legal@edgehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: security@edgehealth.com
Date: Tue, 04 May 2027 11:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — KMS / export-control questions

Ava — thanks for the materials. A few clarifying points from our side:

- EdgeHealth requires that any vendor serving protected health information (PHI) supports customer-managed keys (CMKs) with no escrow by the vendor, or alternatively a BAA that explicitly documents permitted key usage and emergency access procedures.
- We need to confirm any subprocessors that could access plaintext for troubleshooting, analytics, or model optimization. If a subprocessor can access plaintext, we expect contractual constraints and audit evidence (SOC2 type II evidence + role-based access logs).
- Export-control: we need to know the physical/geo location where decryption can occur. If any decryption event happens in a non-US/EU region, we need to assess export implications.

Please provide: (A) a short yes/no on whether Redwood ever persists plaintext outside CMK-encrypted blobs, (B) the list of subprocessors that could access plaintext and a short description of purpose, (C) the geo footprint for decryption events.

If you have a concise DPA paragraph for key custody, that would help speed internal review.

—
Dana Morales
Sr Counsel, Privacy & Security
EdgeHealth


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: legal@edgehealth.com
Date: Wed, 05 May 2027 08:56:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Dataflow mapping & subprocessor reachability — draft answers

Forwarding draft answers for review — writing inline for clarity. Ava / Marissa please edit and I'll finalize.

--- Neha (draft) ---
A) Plaintext persistence: No. Redwood does not persist customer inputs or generated outputs in plaintext outside of customer-controlled encryption boundaries. All production stores that may persist request/response KV or logs are encrypted at rest; customer-specific model caches can be configured to use customer-managed KMS keys. Temporary memory-resident plaintext exists in process during inference for low-latency serving but is never written to disk unless explicitly configured for debugging by the customer.

B) Subprocessors with potential plaintext access:
- OrbitCloud (optional hosting partner): may see plaintext only if the customer chooses to deploy with Orbit-managed keys and enables Orbit-managed hosting; if the customer provides CMKs and we configure wrapping so Redwood never exports unwrapped keys, OrbitCloud cannot decrypt payloads. Purpose: optional region hosting/edge routing.
- Support contractors (redacted vendor name): limited access to logs/tooling for debugging under strict role-based access; require beat-the-door escalation and provide auditable access logs.
- Model optimization partner (analytics): does not have access to raw customer payloads under default config; we only share anonymized aggregated telemetry unless customer opts in for sample-level analysis.

C) Geo footprint for decryption events:
- Default hosted path: decryption occurs in the region where the model serving is provisioned (customer-configurable). We can ensure US-only decryption for EdgeHealth by pinning serving to US regions and using CMKs backed in US-only KMS.

I’ll attach a short KMS custody paragraph for Marissa to polish.

—Neha


From: Dana Morales <legal@edgehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.inference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: security@edgehealth.com, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Thu, 06 May 2027 09:14:00 -07:00

1) OrbitCloud: can Orbit provide a written statement that they do not hold unwrapped customer keys and that they will only have access to plaintext when explicitly configured by the customer? If Redwood can facilitate a short attestation from Orbit that would be ideal.
2) Support contractors: we need the vendor name and SOC2 evidence that covers the relevant subprocessors.
3) Can you provide a redline-friendly DPA paragraph for keys and a proposed addendum that we can circulate internally (one paragraph + a one-page appendix listing subprocessors and the exact data flow).

If you can send an initial draft addendum today we can get it into our procurement queue — otherwise we’ll schedule a short follow-up next week.

—Dana

preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@orchid.ai>, James O'Neill <james.oneill@orchid.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T11:12:00-07:00
Subject: End-of-commitment notice matrix & redline handoff

Hi Marta / James —

Thanks for the time on yesterday's call. Attached is our current MSA redline and a draft order form for the pilot. Key items we flagged for legal/procurement alignment:

- Auto-renew: Redwood's standard stance is a 60-day opt-out notice before the renewal effective date. We saw Orchid's request for a shorter 45-day window in procurement notes — want to understand whether that's for pilot only or enterprise-wide.
- Termination for convenience: current MSA allows termination with 60 days' notice for enterprise engagements. Orchid's procurement suggested 30 days. We can make a concession for pilot projects but need a clearer wind-down and refund mechanism.
- Transition assistance & data handoff: propose 30 days minimum for transfer support if either party terminates for convenience; costs / pro-rated fees to be clarified in order form.

I've copied Karthik and Laura to confirm our recommended positions; Laura, please call out any must-haves for the MSA language. Marta/James, can you confirm procurement's target notice for pilot vs. production? If you prefer, we can propose a split-window (pilot 30–45 days, enterprise 60).

Attachments: MSA_Redline_v3.docx (redline), OrderForm_Orchid_20270502.xlsx (pricing draft)

Next steps: would like to lock on a compromise by EOD Friday May 6 so legal can produce a consolidated redline and we can send DocuSign.

Best,
Ava Chen
Director, Commercial Partnerships
Redwood Inference

--


From: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@orchid.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, James O'Neill <james.oneill@orchid.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T14:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: End-of-commitment notice matrix & redline handoff

Ava —

Thanks for the note and the redline. Quick clarifications from Orchid procurement:

- Auto-renew: Procurement's preference is a 45-day opt-out across the agreement for predictability. For pilots, we were thinking 30 days, but would accept 45 for consistency.
- Termination for convenience: we need the ability to exit pilots with 30 days' notice and enterprise with 45 days. We are not comfortable with 60 days for enterprise without a stronger service commitment.
- Refunds: we expect pro-rated refunds for prepaid amounts less any actual consumed usage. No early-termination penalty for pilots; for enterprise, a modest wind-down fee is acceptable but should be capped at one month's committed fees.

I've attached a PDF with our marked comments (Orchid_MSA_Comments.pdf). Happy to jump on a short call with your legal team to resolve the pilot vs enterprise split.

Best,
Marta Lopez
Head of Procurement
Orchid Analytics

--


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: internal — positions on notice & termination windows

Ava / Laura —

Summarizing recommended positions before we reply to Orchid:

1) Auto-renew: keep enterprise at 60 days (standard operationally), offer 45 days as a concession only if Orchid commits to a 30% prepayment schedule or a minimum annual commitment. For pilots, agree to 30 or 45 days depending on pilot length.
2) Termination for convenience: accept a tiered model — pilot: 30 days with no early termination fee; enterprise: 60 days notice with either (a) pro-rated refund for unused prepaid amounts minus reasonable wind-down costs, or (b) an election for customer to pay one quarter of the committed remaining fees as a buyout. We should aim to cap any wind-down fee to limit disputes.
3) Transition assistance: codify 30 days of minimal transition assistance with optional paid extended support at our standard T&M rates.

Laura — please review and flag mandatory legal language. I'm attaching a short internal summary so we can track positions (internal-redline-summary.txt).

-Karthik
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

--


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T13:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: internal — legal note on termination/renewal language

Karthik / Ava —

Thanks — quick legal notes on the redline:

- Section 8.2 (Termination for Convenience): propose we use a tiered clause. Wording suggestion: "Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon written notice to the other party as follows: (i) for Pilot Agreements, 30 days' prior written notice; (ii) for Enterprise Agreements, 45 days' prior written notice where the Customer has a rolling monthly/quarterly commitment, and 60 days' prior written notice where Customer has annual prepayment or committed capacity." This gives us flexibility operationally.
- Refunds & fees: refunds should be limited to unused prepaid fees less our reasonable wind-down costs (defined as costs incurred to deprovision/custom export, estimated not-to-exceed X% of remaining term). For enterprise buyout, we can agree a capped buyout equal to one month's average monthly committed fees to keep it simple.
- Auto-renew: propose we accept 45 days for enterprise in exchange for a 12-month minimum commitment or minimum spend threshold. Otherwise keep 60 days. For pilots, accept 30 days.
- Transition assistance: require explicit SOW/Order Form to document deliverables and rates for post-termination support. Minimum 30 days' assistance included for all terminations for convenience.

I'll convert these into tracked changes and produce legal-redline-notes.docx; once we have procurement's final ask we should push back with a single clean compromise redline for signature.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference

--


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marta Lopez <marta.lopez@orchid.ai>, James O'Neill <james.oneill@orchid.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>

Marta / James —

Appreciate the quick turnaround. We've digested your comments and Laura has produced suggested language that we think is a fair compromise. High level proposal:

- Auto-renew: Pilot agreements — 30 day opt-out; Enterprise agreements — 60 day opt-out standard. We will accept 45 days for enterprise only if Orchid agrees to a 12-month minimum spend or a 30% prepayment on order execution.
- Termination for convenience: Pilot — 30 days' notice, no termination fee; Enterprise — 45 days' notice if rolling monthly/quarterly commitments, 60 days' notice for annual prepayments/committed capacity. Refunds limited to unused prepaid fees less reasonable wind-down costs; as a simplified option, a capped buyout equal to one month's average committed fees is acceptable.
- Transition assistance: 30 days included; any additional support will be covered at standard T&M rates documented in the SOW.

Attached is a consolidated redline reflecting the language above (MSA_Redline_v4.docx) and a clean order form (OrderForm_Orchid_20270502.xlsx). If this is acceptable, we can push the redline into DocuSign and aim to have countersigned docs within one week.

Subject: Re: internal — positions on notice & termination windows

Ava / Laura —

Summarizing recommended positions before we reply to Orchid:

1) Auto-renew: keep enterprise at 60 days (standard operationally), offer 45 days as a concession only if Orchid commits to a 30% prepayment schedule or a minimum annual commitment. For pilots, agree to 30 or 45 days depending on pilot length.
2) Termination for convenience: accept a tiered model — pilot: 30 days with no early termination fee; enterprise: 60 days notice with either (a) pro-rated refund for unused prepaid amounts minus reasonable wind-down costs, or (b) an election for customer to pay one quarter of the committed remaining fees as a buyout. We should aim to cap any wind-down fee to limit disputes.
3) Transition assistance: codify 30 days of minimal transition assistance with optional paid extended support at our standard T&M rates.

Laura — please review and flag mandatory legal language. I'm attaching a short internal summary so we can track positions (internal-redline-summary.txt).

-Karthik
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

--


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T13:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: internal — legal note on termination/renewal language

Karthik / Ava —

Thanks — quick legal notes on the redline:

- Section 8.2 (Termination for Convenience): propose we use a tiered clause. Wording suggestion: "Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon written notice to the other party as follows: (i) for Pilot Agreements, 30 days' prior written notice; (ii) for Enterprise Agreements, 45 days' prior written notice where the Customer has a rolling monthly/quarterly commitment, and 60 days' prior written notice where Customer has annual prepayment or committed capacity." This gives us flexibility operationally.
- Refunds & fees: refunds should be limited to unused prepaid fees less our reasonable wind-down costs (defined as costs incurred to deprovision/custom export, estimated not-to-exceed X% of remaining term). For enterprise buyout, we can agree a capped buyout equal to one month's average monthly committed fees to keep it simple.
- Auto-renew: propose we accept 45 days for enterprise in exchange for a 12-month minimum commitment or minimum spend threshold. Otherwise keep 60 days. For pilots, accept 30 days.
- Transition assistance: require explicit SOW/Order Form to document deliverables and rates for post-termination support. Minimum 30 days' assistance included for all terminations for convenience.

I'll convert these into tracked changes and produce legal-redline-notes.docx; once we have procurement's final ask we should push back with a single clean compromise redline for signature.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference

--

- Auto-renew: Pilot agreements — 30 day opt-out; Enterprise agreements — 60 day opt-out standard. We will accept 45 days for enterprise only if Orchid agrees to a 12-month minimum spend or a 30% prepayment on order execution.
- Termination for convenience: Pilot — 30 days' notice, no termination fee; Enterprise — 45 days' notice if rolling monthly/quarterly commitments, 60 days' notice for annual prepayments/committed capacity. Refunds limited to unused prepaid fees less reasonable wind-down costs; as a simplified option, a capped buyout equal to one month's average committed fees is acceptable.
- Transition assistance: 30 days included; any additional support will be covered at standard T&M rates documented in the SOW.

title: VPC schematic validation + SLA matrix review
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.patel@edgeworks.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: VPC schematic validation + SLA matrix review

Hi Samir —

Attached is our latest VPC schematic (v3) and a draft SLA matrix that maps service boundaries to SLO targets. Key asks:

- Can your team confirm that the proposed NAT/egress model and IP ranges in the schematic are compatible with EdgeWorks' transit fabric? (see diagram pages 2–3)
- For the control plane peering: do you support an mTLS-protected BGP session over the private L2/L3 link, or do you prefer an L3 GRE+IPsec tunnel?
- Can you confirm whether EdgeWorks can provide an allowlist for the control-plane management IPs and whether there are per-tenant ACL requirements?

Attachments: vpc_schematic_v3.pdf, sla_matrix_proposal.xlsx

Meeting proposal: 1-hour alignment on Thursday 5/6 10:00–11:00 PT — we want a signoff on the connectivity and SLA numbers to finalize the ops runbook.

High-level notes:
- Control plane traffic (k8s API, agent heartbeats) should be routed over the private peering; data-plane inference traffic uses customer egress via NAT/gateway.
- We propose a canary rollout: 10% traffic to private deployment variant for 72 hours, health checks at 30s granularity.

Next steps: confirm peering mode and any IP/port restrictions; Samir, if you can loop in networking l2/l3 owner that would be great.

Thanks,
Ava
Ava Chen
Product Manager, Private Deploy
Redwood Inference
[Attachment: vpc_schematic_v3.pdf]
[Attachment: sla_matrix_proposal.xlsx]

From: Samir Patel <samir.patel@edgeworks.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: EdgeWorks Networking Team <eng@edgeworks.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: VPC schematic validation + SLA matrix review

Ava —

Thanks for sending this over. Quick read-through and initial observations:

- Schematic looks straightforward. The IP ranges you proposed (10.10.0.0/16 for control-plane, 10.20.0.0/16 for data-plane) are fine from our side, but we need the final ASN for the peering and the BGP neighbor IPs to create filters.
- EdgeWorks prefers an mTLS-wrapped BGP session where possible; we can terminate mTLS on our edge routers. GRE+IPsec is supported as fallback but adds complexity for multi-tenant routing.
- For allowlisting: we can provide a short-lived prefix list for the control-plane management block; tell us the maintenance window for route announcements if you plan to shift prefixes during cutover.

I attached our internal checklist with a few additional items (certificate rotation cadence, expected TLS ciphers, and an MTU note for EVPN). Also, regarding SLA: 99.9% for control-plane api responsiveness seems achievable, data-plane inference P99 latency targets will depend on tenant workload — we can baseline after a staged run.

Proposed action: two-track meeting — (1) network peering details with our routing lead (we can do Wed 5/5 AM PT) and (2) SLA alignment with performance engineering (Thu 5/6 as you suggested).

Samir
Samir Patel
VP, Cloud Networking
EdgeWorks
[Attachment: edgeworks_networking_checklist.docx]

From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Patel <samir.patel@edgeworks.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-02T12:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: VPC schematic validation + SLA matrix review

Folks —

Thanks — a few internal points from infra/ops to capture before the alignment meeting:

- Control plane: we want mTLS for the control-plane channel with mutual cert verification and an explicit fingerprint exchange during provisioning. That will let ops rotate certs without a hard cutover.
- Peering failover: please confirm whether EdgeWorks supports dual-backbone paths (we may want two BGP neighbors for resilience).
- NAT translation and egress: we will prefer 1:many SNAT for tenant outbound with per-tenant source ports tracked; if EdgeWorks' transit enforces source-ip preservation for logging, we should preserve a subset of tenant IPs.
- Observability: ensure that NetFlow/sFlow or telemetry hooks can be mirrored to our observability collector during the canary window.

title: SIG / SOC2 evidence: export retention boundaries and jurisdictional options
preview:
From: Mark Rivera <mark.rivera@solarahealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: privacy@solarahealth.com
Date: Fri, 30 Apr 2027 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Admin activity export — jurisdiction / retention question

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. We’re working through the SIG and SOC2 evidence checklist and hit a question about admin activity / audit-log exports. Two quick items we need to confirm before we mark the control as answered: 

1) Does the admin-activity export include per-event timezone-normalized timestamps (UTC) and the original local timezone? We need both for our internal reconciliation.
2) Can you provide a short note on jurisdictional handling — specifically if a customer requests an export that includes activity for users in the EU, can that data be exported out of-region, or is there an on-region packaged export option? Our privacy team needs the wording for the SIG response.

If you can point to a sample manifest or a short statement we can include in the questionnaire that’d be ideal. Thanks — Mark

(attachment: sig-questionnaire.pdf - 320 KB)


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mark Rivera <mark.rivera@solarahealth.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Apr 2027 10:26:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export — jurisdiction / retention question

Hi Mark — great to hear from you and glad the demo was useful. Short answers up front, then details below.

1) Yes — exports include UTC-normalized timestamps and we retain the original timezone string on each event.
2) We support both cross-region exports and a geofenced packaged export. By default hosted exports are generated in the region where the tenant is provisioned; for EU tenants we can produce an on-region sealed bundle.

I’m looping in Kimberly from Legal to provide suggested SIG language and any required caveats. Neha on infra is cc’d for the export manifest sample and the small CSV with field-level examples.

Next steps: Kimberly will draft a short paragraph you can paste into the SIG; Neha will attach an export manifest sample. Let me know if you need the export delivered to an SFTP or a signed Drive link.

Best,
Ava

--
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com
(attachment referenced: export_manifest_sample.csv - stub)


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mark Rivera <mark.rivera@solarahealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Apr 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export — jurisdiction / retention question

Mark — provided below is a suggested SIG / CAIQ response you can paste into the questionnaire. It’s intentionally short and prescriptive. If Solara needs specific contract language we can follow up with legal.

Suggested respondent text (SIG / CAIQ):
"Redwood Inference logs administrative and operator activity at the per-event level, including user identity, action type, resource targeted, and a timezone-normalized timestamp (UTC). The original local timezone string is stored alongside each event for reconciliation and forensic purposes. Export options include: (a) cross-region encrypted export delivered to customer-specified storage endpoints; and (b) an on-region sealed export bundle for tenants provisioned in-region (e.g., EU region) to support data residency requirements. Exports are authenticated and integrity-protected; access to export delivery is logged and retained per the customer's retention policy."

Retention note: our hosted default retention for admin activity is 13 months, with configurable shorter/longer retention windows available on Dedicated and Private deployments. Exports are generated as immutable, signed bundles and include a manifest describing event counts, time window, and checksum.

If you want, I can add a short clause about exceptional legal requests (e.g., subpoenas) — we typically refer to the contract/legal team for those scenarios.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Privacy & Compliance | Redwood Inference


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mark Rivera <mark.rivera@solarahealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, 02 May 2027 15:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export — jurisdiction / retention question

Hi Mark — attaching a tiny sample manifest and an example CSV row to show the fields we include. Notes below.

Attachment stubs:
- export_manifest_sample.csv (attachment: export_manifest_sample.csv - 4 KB)
- field_legend.txt (attachment: field_legend.txt - 1 KB)

Key technical points: 
- Fields included per event: event_id, actor_id (internal id), actor_email (if available), action, resource_type, resource_id, original_timestamp, utc_timestamp, timezone, request_id, result_status, kv_cache_key (nullable).
- Timestamps: utc_timestamp is ISO8601 with Z; original_timestamp holds the inbound timestamp recorded by the origin service and includes the timezone string.
- Export packaging: we produce gzipped NDJSON (line-delimited JSON) for large windows; for smaller windows we generate CSV. Every bundle includes: manifest.json (summary line counts/sha256), bundle.sig (detached signature), and README.md with redaction notes.
- Jurisdictional handling: for tenants marked as EU-resident we default exports to the eu-central region. Cross-region exports are available only after customer explicit consent captured in the export request form and an export-level consent flag is recorded on the manifest.
- Retention controls: retention is enforced at the storage layer; on Dedicated/Private deployments administrators can request retention changes via the Console or API. For hosted tenants we require a signed request for retention rates outside the default SLA.

Happy to walk through specifics on a quick call if your privacy team prefers — we can run a sample export and demonstrate the manifest / signature validation.

-neha

Neha Kapoor
SRE, Inference Platform | Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com

Date: Fri, 30 Apr 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export — jurisdiction / retention question

Mark — provided below is a suggested SIG / CAIQ response you can paste into the questionnaire. It’s intentionally short and prescriptive. If Solara needs specific contract language we can follow up with legal.

Suggested respondent text (SIG / CAIQ):
"Redwood Inference logs administrative and operator activity at the per-event level, including user identity, action type, resource targeted, and a timezone-normalized timestamp (UTC). The original local timezone string is stored alongside each event for reconciliation and forensic purposes. Export options include: (a) cross-region encrypted export delivered to customer-specified storage endpoints; and (b) an on-region sealed export bundle for tenants provisioned in-region (e.g., EU region) to support data residency requirements. Exports are authenticated and integrity-protected; access to export delivery is logged and retained per the customer's retention policy."

Retention note: our hosted default retention for admin activity is 13 months, with configurable shorter/longer retention windows available on Dedicated and Private deployments. Exports are generated as immutable, signed bundles and include a manifest describing event counts, time window, and checksum.

If you want, I can add a short clause about exceptional legal requests (e.g., subpoenas) — we typically refer to the contract/legal team for those scenarios.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Privacy & Compliance | Redwood Inference


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mark Rivera <mark.rivera@solarahealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, 02 May 2027 15:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export — jurisdiction / retention question

Hi Mark — attaching a tiny sample manifest and an example CSV row to show the fields we include. Notes below.

Attachment stubs:
- export_manifest_sample.csv (attachment: export_manifest_sample.csv - 4 KB)
- field_legend.txt (attachment: field_legend.txt - 1 KB)

Key technical points: 
- Fields included per event: event_id, actor_id (internal id), actor_email (if available), action, resource_type, resource_id, original_timestamp, utc_timestamp, timezone, request_id, result_status, kv_cache_key (nullable).
- Timestamps: utc_timestamp is ISO8601 with Z; original_timestamp holds the inbound timestamp recorded by the origin service and includes the timezone string.
- Export packaging: we produce gzipped NDJSON (line-delimited JSON) for large windows; for smaller windows we generate CSV. Every bundle includes: manifest.json (summary line counts/sha256), bundle.sig (detached signature), and README.md with redaction notes.
- Jurisdictional handling: for tenants marked as EU-resident we default exports to the eu-central region. Cross-region exports are available only after customer explicit consent captured in the export request form and an export-level consent flag is recorded on the manifest.
- Retention controls: retention is enforced at the storage layer; on Dedicated/Private deployments administrators can request retention changes via the Console or API. For hosted tenants we require a signed request for retention rates outside the default SLA.

Happy to walk through specifics on a quick call if your privacy team prefers — we can run a sample export and demonstrate the manifest / signature validation.

-neha

Neha Kapoor
SRE, Inference Platform | Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com

title: Embeddings packaging, per-vector vs per-token billing, and bulk discounting
preview:
From: Alex Rivera <alex@bluewave.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Sun, 25 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Embeddings — billing model, dims, and model compatibility

Hi Ava,

Thanks for taking a look here. Quick intro: I lead ML infra at BlueWave AI — we embed product text for retrieval and personalization (~2–5M vectors/month today, projecting up to 40M in 6 months). We’re evaluating Redwood for hosted embeddings. A few concrete questions that would help us scope cost/packaging: 

- Do you bill embeddings per-vector or per-token? If per-vector, how do you treat different dimensionalities (e.g., 512 / 1536 / 4096)? If per-token, is that input tokens only or both input+creation tokens?
- Which embedding models are supported in the hosted plan? Are there clear tiered price differences between smaller models and high-dim models?
- What do your bulk/commit discounts look like? Example: our initial POC would be ~10M vectors over 3 months, followed by steady-state 20–40M/mo if things go well.
- Any packaging options (prepaid vector credits, blended meters, or included free tier) you recommend for a startup scaling like ours?

If you have a rate card or short doc you can share, that’d be great. Also happy to hop on a 30–45m call.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Rivera
Head of ML Infrastructure, BlueWave AI
alex@bluewave.ai
+1 415-555-0192

[Attachment: brief_product_overview.pdf (stub)]

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Alex Rivera <alex@bluewave.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 26 Apr 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Embeddings — billing model, dims, and model compatibility

Alex — great to meet you (virtually) and thanks for the context. I attached our current embeddings rate card and a quick notes doc. High level answers below — I kept this focused so you can paste into internal models.

1) Billing unit: per-vector is our primary billing abstraction for embeddings on the hosted API. That means you’re billed one unit per returned vector. We separate text preprocessing (input tokens) billing only when customers use our text-generation or tokenization-heavy pipelines; for pure embeddings use, vector units are the right way to model cost.

2) Dimensions and model families: we support multiple embedding families (lightweight 512-d, standard 1536-d, and high-capacity 4096-d variants). Standard pricing on the rate card is anchored to the 1536-d family. Pricing model: base per-vector price for 1536-d, and then multipliers for other dims: 512-d = 0.7x of base, 4096-d = 1.7x. This keeps billing predictable while reflecting compute delta.

3) Model support: for hosted customers we currently provide the curated list in the attachment (Redwood_Embeddings_Rate_Card_v2.pdf). Short list: RW-Embed-Standard (1536-d), RW-Embed-Small (512-d), RW-Embed-High (4096-d). We also support a few community variants for compatibility but those are quoted case-by-case for SLA/throughput reasons.

4) Bulk/commit discounts & packaging: we offer three common options — (a) prepaid vector credits (one-time top-ups usable over 12 months), (b) monthly committed volume discounts (tiered by monthly vectors), and (c) enterprise blended-rate contracts for very large steady-state usage. Example anchor: standard list price is $0.00030 per 1536-d vector; committed tiers can go as low as $0.00018 per vector for 10M+ vectors/month with a 12-month minimum. Prepaid credit pricing usually gives similar / slightly better effective rates depending on term length.

5) Quick POC suggestion: for a 3-month 10M vector POC we typically recommend prepaid credits plus a small initial reserved capacity bump so you see consistent latency. That combination prevents surprises as you ramp.

I’m looping in Karthik from Pricing/Partnerships to help with sketch pricing and a formal quote. I also attached a short comparison sheet that maps dims -> multiplier and an FAQ on model eligibility.

Next steps: would you like a short pricing sketch (sample invoice) and a proposed PO? If yes I can have a draft to you in 24–48 hours. If you prefer a call, I can book Karthik and Marissa (finance) for midweek.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com
[Attachment: Redwood_Embeddings_Rate_Card_v2.pdf (stub); link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcD-example]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Alex Rivera <alex@bluewave.ai>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 27 Apr 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Embeddings — billing model, dims, and model compatibility

Thanks Ava — looping Marissa for commercial/discount guidance. Alex, quick clarifying Qs so we can sketch a tailored commercial:  

- Is the 10M vectors for the POC concentrated in a single month or spread over 3 months?
- What latency SLOs matter to you (tail p95/p99 targets)? If you need reserved capacity we’ll price a small monthly reserve on top of vector units.
- Any regulatory constraints (data residency) that would push this to a dedicated/private deployment?

We can draft two options: an upfront prepaid credit package (best for short POC) and a 12mo committed-tier invoice (best if you want predictable unit economics). Marissa — if you could share the typical tiered discount table (we can tweak for this account) that will speed things up.

Thanks,
Karthik
Director, GTM Pricing, Redwood Inference

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Alex Rivera <alex@bluewave.ai>
Date: Tue, 27 Apr 2027 11:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Embeddings — billing model, dims, and model compatibility

Alex — quick note from finance/comm ops. Attaching the commercial tiers workbook and a short note on packaging options. Key items: 

- Tiered discounts (monthly committed vectors): 0–1M (list), 1–5M (5% off), 5–20M (20% off), 20M+ (40% off) — these are illustrative; final pricing will depend on term and payment cadence.
- Prepaid credits: we can offer 6% extra vector credit on top of prepayment for 3mo term, 12% extra for 12mo.
- High-dim surcharge: any vector config > 2048 dims mapped to the 4096-d family multiplier (1.7x) as Ava noted; for mixed-dim workloads we apply weighted averaging on consumed vector units.
- For the POC: we can do a prepaid 10M vector bundle with a one-time onboarding credit (technical support hours) and a 30% commitment discount on top of the prepaid uplift for first-time enterprise clients.

I’ll mark the workbook as "POC suggested" and share a version with editable fields if you want to run numbers.

Cheers,
Marissa Cole
Head of Commercial Operations, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: Embeddings_Commercial_Tiers.xlsx (stub)]

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Alex Rivera <alex@bluewave.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 28 Apr 2027 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Embeddings — billing model, dims, and model compatibility

Alex — summarizing agreed next steps so we’re aligned: 

- Redwood will send a POC quote (prepaid 10M vector credits, 3mo valid) at the committed discount outlined by Marissa.
- We’ll include an onboarding block (8 hours of engineering support) and reserved capacity seat to stabilize p99 latency during ramp.
- If BlueWave signs a 12-month commit (20M+ avg/mo) we’ll lock into the blended enterprise rate we discussed and provide separate SLAs for latency and data residency.

I’ll have the formal quote + draft SOW and a DocuSign link to you by end of day tomorrow. Once you’ve had a look we can schedule a quick 30m call to walk the commercial and the onboarding plan.

Thanks for moving quickly on this — excited to partner.

Best,
Ava Chen
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com

To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>

Subject: Cofunded trial credits for CloudMarket listing - routing ask

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the intro call yesterday. NimbleWorks is ready to list Redwood on CloudMarket next month and we'd like to co-fund trials for our mutual customers. Before we finalize the marketplace listing, legal and billing asked for a short routing plan: how customers redeem SI-sponsored credits vs Redwood promotional credits, and whether credits can be top-up applied in the marketplace flow.

Attached is a draft "credit-allocation-matrix.xlsx" showing three scenarios (new customers, proof-of-concept, and expansion pilots) and proposed redemption windows (30/60/90 days). Can you confirm Redwood can: 1) accept partner-issued voucher codes that map to a marketplace trial product, and 2) apply partner cofunding first, with Redwood covering any remaining credits up to the trial cap?

If that flow works, we can get procurement to sign the co-funding agreement and push the DocuSign envelope. If not, please advise what changes you need (routing by HubSpot deal id, different expiration, etc.).

Thanks — looking to finalize by next Tuesday to meet marketplace deadlines.

Best,
Maya
Partnerships Manager, NimbleWorks

Maya —

Appreciate the matrix; clear and very helpful. Short answers below, longer notes after:

1) Partner-issued voucher codes: yes, we can support external partner voucher codes via the marketplace SKU flow, provided we receive the code list and mapping to HubSpot deal ids before listing.
2) Routing order: we can prioritize partner cofunding first, then Redwood credits to top up to the trial cap. We implement this via a two-step redemption call (partner-code validate -> apply partner credit; if remaining balance, apply redwood-promocode).

- Voucher expiry must be a hard date; our marketplace instrument enforces expiration server-side.
