title: Re: Hosted API — data residency, retention, and erasure details (GreenBridge)
preview:
From: Lucas Rivera <security@greenbridge.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Hosted API — data residency / retention / erasure questions

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the demo yesterday. Our InfoSec team has a short set of clarifying questions about the hosted API around data residency, retention and deletion before we proceed to procurement. Can you confirm the following for GreenBridge's evaluation?

1) Where is customer data stored by default (region / country)? Are we able to request EU-only or specific-region residency for a hosted account?
2) What categories of data does Redwood persist (full request/response payloads, logs, billing metadata, metrics, cached KV/state)? For each category, please state default retention window and whether it can be shortened to 0 days.
3) How are backups and disaster recovery snapshots handled? What is the backup retention period and deletion behavior after a user-initiated erase?
4) Describe the deletion/erasure process for hosted accounts. Is deletion synchronous? Do you provide an audit trail proving data removal?
5) Are there any derived artifacts you keep (embeddings, telemetry aggregates) and how are they treated under erasure requests?

If you have a short PDF with the answers & references to your docs or SOC2 artefacts, that would be very helpful. We need this for our procurement checklist by Monday.

Best,
Lucas
Security Engineer, GreenBridge Health

--
GreenBridge Health | security@greenbridge.com


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
To: Lucas Rivera <security@greenbridge.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 11:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API — data residency / retention / erasure questions

Hi Lucas — thanks for the clear list. Below are concise answers; I attached a one-page PDF summary and an extract from our SOC 2 report for evidence. If you want, we can schedule a quick call to walk through any item.

1) Data residency (default / options)
- Default: For new hosted accounts, request/response data and associated metadata are stored in the region you select at account creation. For US customers the default region is us-west-2 (Oregon).
- EU option: We offer EU-hosted tenancy (eu-west-1) for customers who require EU-only residency. We can pin hosted accounts to a region at onboarding; cross-region replication only occurs if explicitly enabled for multi-region apps.

2) Categories persisted & retention windows (defaults and opt-down)
- Request/Response payloads (API inputs and generated outputs): default retention 30 days for debugging and quality monitoring. Customers may opt out at account level to set retention to 0 days for prompt/response content.
- Operational logs / request metadata (timestamps, model used, token counts, latency): default retention 90 days. These are retained for billing, troubleshooting and usage analytics. We support shortening to 30 days on request for customers with stricter needs; 0 days for operational logs is not supported because we need minimal metadata for billing and anti-abuse, but we can pseudonymize retained fields on request.
- Audit trail / admin logs (console logins, admin actions): retained 365 days by default; this supports security investigations and compliance. Can be shortened via enterprise contract in Dedicated/Private offers.
- Cached KV / prefix caches (server-side model caches to speed serving): ephemeral; default eviction window is minutes-to-hours depending on traffic. No persistent long-term storage of user-submitted context in caches.

3) Backups and DR snapshots
- Backups: nightly encrypted snapshots of configuration/state (not full prompt libraries) are taken and retained for up to 90 days.
- Snapshots may include system metadata required to restore service (routing state, model version mappings). We do not store full request/response logs in backups beyond the active retention windows described above.
- Erasure behavior: user-initiated deletions remove primary copies immediately (see below); backups are sanitized on a rolling schedule and the platform's backup retention policy means remnants in snapshots may persist up to 90 days, after which they are cryptographically deleted by key rotation and storage reclamation jobs.

4) Deletion / erasure process
- Self-service: Account admins can trigger data purges from the Redwood Console (Account -> Data Controls -> Purge). This initiates immediate removal of primary stored request/response records and marks records for removal from active indices.
- Asynchronous cleanup: Deletion is acknowledged synchronously in the UI, while background jobs delete related artifacts (indices, caches, and references). The primary deletion completes within minutes; full propagation (including queued analytics and backups) completes within 72 hours for primary systems and up to 90 days for backups as noted above.
- Audit trail & confirmation: We generate an audit entry for the purge action (who/when) and provide a deletion token you can use as proof. For enterprise customers we can provide a signed attestation and a log export showing the deletion event timestamp.

5) Derived artifacts (embeddings, telemetry)
- Embeddings derived from customer content are treated as customer data. By default embeddings are retained 30 days; customers may request deletion or retention = 0.
- Aggregated telemetry (anonymized usage statistics, performance histograms) are retained for product analytics and are not reversible to customer content. If you require the removal of a tenant-level aggregate in a contracted environment, we can discuss a contract amendment.

Encryption & access controls (quick notes)
- All data at rest is encrypted (AES-256) and in transit (TLS1.3). Customer data is logically separated per account. Access to raw customer content is limited to a small set of service engineers and only for incident triage; access is logged and gated by JIT approval.

Attachments: the PDF includes a short table with retention windows and links to the console controls. The SOC2 extract shows our control mapping for data deletion and logging.

If you need explicit language for your contract or procurement form, I can provide a one-line clause + appendix wording.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0182

Attachment(s): redwood-data-retention-summary.pdf, redwood-soc2-extract.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
To: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 12:15:00 -08:00
Subject: Fwd: Hosted API — data residency / retention / erasure questions

Folks — forwarding GreenBridge's questions + my draft answers (attached above). Please quick-review backup / snapshot wording and the 90-day backup retention comment. Procurement wants a clear statement on how backups are sanitized after a purge (they expect 'residuals removed within 90 days' phrasing). Can you confirm that’s accurate and whether we should call out key-rotation as the technical mechanism?

Also, Jonas — can you confirm whether we can offer 30-day operational logs instead of 90 as a baseline for mid-market (and whether that requires an account-level flag)?

Thanks,
Ava


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 14:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Hosted API — data residency / retention / erasure questions

Ava — quick responses inline: 
- Backup wording: 'Residuals in backups are removed via scheduled snapshot expiration and cryptographic key rotation; customers should expect residual data in encrypted backups to be unrecoverable within 90 days of a purge.' That aligns with how our storage providers and key rotation processes work. Good to call out key rotation.
- Operational logs: we can offer 30-day retention for operational logs on request for hosted accounts; it’s an account-level flag set during onboarding (no infra change). 0 days for operational logs is not feasible for billing/abuse detection.

Jonas — please chime in if you'd like any edits; otherwise this is good to send to Lucas.

-Priyom

title: Procurement lane decision: Marketplace listing, Private Offer, or Direct Redwood contract
preview:
From: Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T14:21:00Z
Subject: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Procurement is asking for a clear recommendation on whether we should buy Redwood through a cloud marketplace (AWS/Azure/GCP), request a Marketplace Private Offer, or go through a direct Redwood contract (MSA + PO). Their main concerns: speed of procurement, invoice and PO flow, discounts/credits, and any impact on support/SLA and co-sell benefits.

High-level context: Nimble Health is piloting a 2-month PoC (approx. $18k spend) and wants the option to scale to a reserved capacity commitment if things look good. The procurement team prefers marketplace buys for quick approval, but our legal team may request an MSA for anything above a 90-day pilot.

Can you outline pros/cons and practical timelines for each path? Also—any gotchas around co-sell credits or channel incentives we should be aware of?

Appreciate a short, actionable breakdown we can hand to procurement.

Thanks,
Samira
Samira Khan
Head of Procurement, Nimble Health
Samira.khan@nimblehealth.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

[Attachment stub: Nimble_SOW_draft.pdf]

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Cc: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-02T09:07:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Samira — thanks for the clear context. Below is a concise, procurement-ready comparison and my recommendation based on Nimble's PoC -> scale profile. I’ve attached a short pricing matrix and a checklist we use for AWS Private Offers.

1) Cloud Marketplace (public listing)
- Speed: Fastest. Customer can purchase via existing cloud contract or consolidated invoice within 1–7 business days once procurement approves.
- Billing: Billed via the cloud provider’s invoice to the customer (consolidated cloud bill).
- Pricing & discounts: Standard list pricing on the listing. Limited ability to apply negotiated discounts — typically managed via Provider or Marketplace promotions, not line-item custom pricing.
- Support/SLA: Standard Redwood hosted SLA applies, but there are limits on contractual addenda via the marketplace. For pilots, this is often acceptable.
- Co-sell impact: Marketplace listing makes it straightforward for cloud partner teams to attribute usage, but co-sell incentive mechanics vary by partner and may require a private offer to capture special credits.
- Best when: Customer needs immediate procurement simplicity and is OK with standard pricing. Good for pilots and low-friction PoC purchases.

2) Marketplace Private Offer (MPP) / Cloud Private Offer
- Speed: Moderate. Setting up a private offer requires coordination between buyer procurement, cloud sales, and Redwood (usually 5–20 business days depending on approvals).
- Billing: Still billed through the cloud provider (customer sees charges on cloud bill), but pricing/discounts can be customized in the private offer.
- Pricing & discounts: Supports negotiated discounts, custom SKUs, and term-based commitments. Allows us to reflect a negotiated pilot price or scale pricing while keeping the billing path through the marketplace.
- Support/SLA: You can include a reference to the Redwood SLA, and commercial terms are captured in the private offer; however, legal teams sometimes still prefer an MSA for broader terms.
- Co-sell impact: Best path to preserve marketplace billing while enabling co-sell crediting and partner incentives — cloud partner needs to sign off on the private offer to make it co-sell-eligible.
- Best when: You want marketplace invoicing but need negotiated price/terms (pilot discounts, transition to commitment).

3) Direct Redwood Contract (MSA + PO / Redwood Invoice)
- Speed: Slowest. Negotiation of MSA and commercial terms typically 2–8 weeks (can be longer if custom legal terms).
- Billing: Redwood issues invoices directly (or can accept customer PO per the MSA).
- Pricing & discounts: Full flexibility to negotiate. Can include committed discounts, reserved capacity, and custom SLAs.
- Support/SLA: Full contract flexibility (dedicated support add-ons, explicit uptime targets, breach remedies).
- Co-sell impact: Co-sell crediting requires coordination; direct contracts can still be co-sell-enabled but may not flow through marketplace incentive systems automatically — we typically set a co-sell arrangement in parallel.
- Best when: Customer needs contractual protections, custom SLAs, or wants Redwood to be the invoicing party (finance or compliance reasons). Also recommended for long-term reserved capacity deals.

Recommendation for Nimble Health (PoC -> scale):
- For the 2-month PoC (~$18k) we recommend Marketplace public listing if procurement wants the fastest route and doesn’t need custom legal terms.
- If you want a discounted pilot price or anticipate rolling the pilot into a committed reserved capacity (and want to keep unified cloud billing), opt for a Marketplace Private Offer — it balances speed and negotiated pricing.
- If Nimble plans for immediate reserved capacity, data isolation, or custom SLAs, pursue a direct MSA and PO (longer timeline, more flexible commercial terms).

Next steps I propose:
- If you want to move quickly: Proceed via public marketplace (I can provide the listing link and PO guidance).
- If you want discount and cloud-billing continuity: I’ll open the Private Offer path and coordinate with the AWS account team (I’ll need the procurement contact and PO template).
- If legal needs an MSA first: I’ll send a draft MSA and our standard SOW (DocuSign link below).

Links & attachments: pricing matrix and AWS Private Offer checklist attached. DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/link/to/draft-msa

Happy to jump on a 20-min call with procurement/legal to walk their teams through each lane — what time tomorrow works?

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0199
[Attachment stubs: Redwood_pricing_matrix_v3.pdf; AWS_private_offer_checklist.xlsx]

From: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Date: 2026-12-03T11:32:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Thanks Aditya — that breakdown helps. A couple quick clarifications:
- If we do a private offer via AWS, does the invoice still flow through AWS and satisfy our existing PO process (i.e., we get a single consolidated cloud invoice)?
- For direct Redwood invoicing, can Redwood accept a standard Nimble PO and net-30 terms? We typically require PO + net-30 for vendor payments.
- Support: for pilots we usually accept standard support, but if we commit to reserved capacity we will want a defined escalation path and a named TAM. Is that something Redwood can include in the MSA/SOW?

If we can get quick answers to the above, procurement can confirm a preference.

—Marty

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>, Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-05T17:48:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Marty — quick answers below and a recommended timeline: 

1) Private Offer invoicing: Yes — invoices are routed through AWS (or chosen cloud) and appear on your consolidated cloud bill. Private Offers allow custom pricing while keeping the cloud billing path intact. That satisfies most procurement PO reconciliation requirements.

2) Direct Redwood invoicing + PO: We can accept a Nimble PO and standard net-30 terms. We’ll include a payment clause in the MSA that aligns to PO processes. I’ve attached a draft MSA and SOW for your review (Redwood_MSA_draft_2026-12.docx).

3) Named TAM / support for reserved capacity: Yes — for committed/reserved customers we include a named Technical Account Manager and defined escalation SLAs in the SOW. We can scope response times and on-call windows in the MSA addendum.

Suggested timelines (typical):
- Public marketplace listing (purchase path ready): 1–7 business days from procurement approval.
- Marketplace Private Offer: 5–20 business days depending on cloud partner approvals and contract review.
- Direct MSA + PO (negotiation + signatures): 2–8 weeks depending on legal review depth.

If procurement wants to move fast, I recommend public marketplace for the PoC. If you want the pilot price locked and a clean transition to commitment while keeping your cloud billing, let me start the Private Offer workflow and I’ll loop in the AWS account team.

Next step: I’ll open the Private Offer intake if you confirm procurement contact and their PO template. Alternatively I can send the public listing link and a one-line PO sample for the quick path.

Best regards,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0199
[Attachments: Redwood_MSA_draft_2026-12.docx]
